headline: Septoplasty — medical necessity (Cigna)
denial_letter_text:
Dear Plan Member,

We received your provider's request on 10/24/2026 for Septoplasty related to Deviated nasal septum with obstruction (J34.2). After review, we cannot approve this request.

Our review applied Cigna Medical Coverage Policies and Milliman Care Guidelines (MCG). The documentation submitted does not demonstrate that the service is medically necessary for this member at this time. Septoplasty is not considered evidence-based for Deviated nasal septum with obstruction (J34.2) under current medical policy. Published criteria do not support authorization.

Specifically, the information provided does not satisfy the utilization-management requirements associated with not evidence based. The clinical notes reviewed did not include sufficient detail to confirm that plan criteria were met. The requesting provider may submit additional records, but the current file does not support approval.

If your provider wishes to discuss this determination, they may contact our physician review line. Any discussion must occur within the timeframe described in your plan materials.

You have the right to appeal this determination. To file a standard appeal, send a written request and supporting medical records within 180 days of the date of this letter. Include your member identification number and the service reference shown on your explanation of benefits.

Sincerely,

Cigna Health Care Management
clinical_context: The 34-year-old member has Deviated nasal septum with obstruction (J34.2). He has been treated under commercial coverage with Septoplasty recommended as the next appropriate step. Failed intranasal steroids 4 months; CT septal deviation 80%. This directly contradicts the insurer's position that not evidence based bars coverage: the chart documents dates, doses, and objective findings that were submitted with the original request submitted in Q1 2026 but are not reflected in the denial letter's rationale. Treating clinicians note ongoing functional impairment and avoidable harm if his care is delayed further.
